name: Mali
experiences: Musée National du Mali and Bamako's Niger-side city life
Great Mosque of Djenné and mud-brick old-town lanes
Timbuktu manuscript-house visits and Saharan skyline
Dogon escarpment village walks with local guides
Seasonal Niger pinasse travel and classic bush-taxi logistics
avoid: March to May gets brutally hot in Djenné, Dogon Country and Timbuktu, so walking days feel shorter and midday sightseeing becomes draining.
June to September brings rain and washouts around Mopti and Bandiagara; roads slow down even as river levels improve for pinasses.

Conflict and banditry risks remain serious in northern Mali and can also change around Mopti-region routes, so never assume a Timbuktu or Dogon transfer is routine without fresh local security advice.
stopoverNote: Bamako (BKO) is the only practical international gateway. Most independent routes use Bamako for the first night, then head east by bush taxi to Djenné and Dogon Country, while Timbuktu works best as a separate add-on by domestic flight or seasonal pinasse rather than a casual overland detour.

cities.name: Bamako
cities.notes: Capital gateway for the National Museum, artisan markets, Niger River evenings, and the easiest place to sort cash, SIMs and onward Mali transport.

cities.name: Djenné
cities.notes: UNESCO mud-brick town for the Great Mosque, low-rise rooftops, market-day energy and one of West Africa's most distinctive historic skylines.

cities.name: Timbuktu
cities.notes: Legendary Saharan city for manuscript houses, Sankoré and Djinguereber exteriors, edge-of-desert light, and transport that always depends on current security.

cities.name: Dogon Country (Bandiagara)
cities.notes: Escarpment base for guided village walks, toguna shelters, granaries and cliff-country views around Bandiagara, Sangha and nearby Dogon communities.

itinerary.sim: Orange Mali and Malitel are the easiest prepaid picks in Bamako; budget roughly ₹280–₹700 for a starter pack with enough data for maps and WhatsApp, but expect weaker speeds once you push toward Dogon Country and the north.

days.theme: Land in the capital, sort logistics, and start with Mali's best museum and easiest first-night food scene.

activities.name: Registered taxi from Modibo Keita International Airport into central Bamako
Musée National du Mali foreign-adult entry
Marché des Artisans browse for textiles, bogolan and woodwork
Taxi hops between the museum, artisan market and Point G / Koulouba viewpoints

days.theme: Keep Bamako practical and flexible with river time, market energy, and ticketing for the harder northern legs.

activities.name: Grand Marché / Marché Rose walking browse
Short Niger River pirogue ride or sunset boat share near the city
Parc National du Mali stroll and café stop
Domestic-flight booking or reconfirmation for the Timbuktu sector when operating

days.theme: Roll in from Sévaré or Mopti, climb to a legal rooftop view, and spend the day inside Djenné's mud-built masterpiece of a townscape.

activities.name: Bush taxi or shared minibus seat from Sévaré / Mopti to Djenné plus the Bani ferry crossing
Great Mosque of Djenné exterior rooftop / photo access arranged through a local house or guide
Old-town clay-lane walking circuit around the mosque quarter
Local guide contribution for the Monday market or town orientation

days.theme: Use Bandiagara as the escarpment base, then walk into the cliff villages with the guide system that makes Dogon visits work.

activities.name: Bush taxi from Mopti / Sévaré to Bandiagara
Mandatory local guide registration for Dogon village access
Kani Kombolé or Teli escarpment village walk with guide
Village entry taxes / cliff-granary contributions

days.theme: Give Dogon Country a second day for a deeper village-to-village walk, slower viewpoints, and onward transport prep back toward Mopti.

activities.name: Shared vehicle toward Sangha or Ende trailheads
Second-day Dogon guide for granaries, toguna shelters and fields below the escarpment
Escarpment viewpoint sunset or short self-paced village walk
Bush taxi back toward Sévaré / Mopti for onward flight or boat logistics

days.theme: Treat Timbuktu as a special add-on: fly or boat in, then focus on manuscripts and the old scholarly quarters.

activities.name: Domestic flight from Bamako or Mopti / Sévaré to Timbuktu when operating
Taxi from Timbuktu airport or Kabara arrival point into town
Mamma Haidara Library or similar family manuscript-house visit donation
Sankoré Mosque and old-town exterior walk

days.theme: Keep the second day slow and atmospheric with mosque exteriors, market lanes, and the transport decisions that define a Mali north leg.

activities.name: Djinguereber Mosque exterior and market-lane walk with local guide
Optional camel ride or sunset outing into the edge dunes
Kabara port excursion or seasonal pinasse arrival/departure logistics
Tea stop and souvenir browse in the Timbuktu market

connections.method: Long-distance bush taxi or minibus to Sévaré / Mopti, then a shared taxi and ferry into Djenné; this is the classic overland route
Shared taxi back via Mopti / Sévaré, then onward bush taxi to Bandiagara or Sangha; easiest to break in Mopti if you leave Djenné late
Return by road to Sévaré / Mopti, then take a domestic flight when operating or a seasonal Niger pinasse to Kabara during higher-water months
Domestic flight is the only time-efficient and usually safer exit; overland returns are much longer and more security-sensitive

extras: Best timing is November to February for cooler, drier air in Bamako, Djenné and Dogon Country, while Niger River levels can still support some seasonal pinasse services toward Timbuktu.

Travel advisory: northern Mali remains conflict-sensitive, and Timbuktu, Gao, Kidal plus some Mopti-region roads can face sudden restrictions or escorts. Recheck current government advisories and vetted local operator advice before booking any northern leg.
Bush taxis are the backbone of the south and center, but they leave when full and daylight timings matter; avoid improvising long road transfers after dark.
Card acceptance is inconsistent outside larger Bamako hotels, so carry cash for museum tickets, mosque-view rooftops, village taxes, and guide fees.
Dress modestly around mosques and ask before photographing people, rooftops, or manuscript collections.
